Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Let me analyze the structure:
- Row 45 has a horizontal red line from col 9 to col 54 (with a gap at col 30 where the white cursor is)
- X-shaped pixels on the left (below row 45, left of cursor)
- Diamond-shaped pixels on the right (below row 45, right of cursor)
- 97 non-row-45 red pixels + 43 row-45 red pixels = 140 total
The X pixels form an X shape centered at approximately (48, 18).
The diamond pixels form a diamond shape centered at approximately (48, 45).
Looking at the scattered red dots above row 45:
- (6, 6), (21, 21), (27, 9), (39, 21), (39, 27) - left side
- (6, 45), (15, 51), (30, 48), (33, 33), (36, 45) - right side
These are likely target dots to be collected.
The charcoal 3x3 squares are also targets:
(6, 6), (6, 45), (15, 51), (21, 21), (27, 9), (30, 48), (33, 33), (38, 20)
Wait, looking at the target positions:
- (5, 5) to (7, 7) - center at (6, 6)
- (5, 44) to (7, 46) - center at (6, 45)
- (14, 50) to (16, 52) - center at (15, 51)
- (20, 20) to (22, 22) - center at (21, 21)
- (26, 8) to (28, 10) - center at (27, 9)
- (29, 47) to (31, 49) - center at (30, 48)
- (32, 32) to (34, 34) - center at (33, 33)
- (38, 20) to (40, 22) - center at (39, 21)
And the red scattered dots include:
(6, 6), (6, 45), (15, 51), (21, 21), (27, 9), (30, 48), (33, 33), (39, 21)
These are exactly the centers of the 8 charcoal squares! So the red dots mark where the charcoal squares are centered.
So the targets are the 8 charcoal 3x3 squares. The structure (horizontal line with X and diamond) needs to be moved so that its arms/edges pass through these squares.
But wait - there's only ONE structure here, not multiple like in level 2. The white cursor is at (45, 30), which is on the horizontal line. This is a single structure.
Let me probe with a RIGHT move to see how the structure moves.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Probe: move RIGHT once to see what changes
action(['RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
